heading: Overview
content: This Google Dev Library article documents the amirisback/automated-build-android-app-with-github-action GitHub repository by Faisal Amir (Muhammad Faisal Amir). The project demonstrates how to automate building Android APKs and App Bundles (AAB) using GitHub Actions workflows. It covers the project's GitHub Action script YAML, how to use GitHub Workflows, automated build of AAB in release mode, automated build of APK in both release and debug modes, using Bundletool to derive a universal APK from an AAB, downloading and uploading build artifacts through GitHub Actions, and clearing old artifacts by naming convention.

heading: Version / Release Notes
content: The latest documented release is version_release = 2.2.7. Its changelog updates the Target SDK to 36, refreshes the GitHub Action script, updates to the latest Android Studio version, updates to the latest Gradle version, updates to the latest Kotlin version, updates the GitHub Action script again, and adds both Bash and Batch build scripts. The article's sample artifact naming convention is ${date_today} - ${repository_name} - ${playstore_name} - APK(s) release generated. The workflow was tested against a private repository and successfully produced both App Bundle(s) and APK artifacts. It can also be run locally through a .run configuration on a developer's own machine. The article links to the full GitHub Actions workflow code, plus further reading on securely building and signing an Android app with GitHub Actions, using GitHub Actions to automate Android app development, and updating the Java setup action version used in CI.

heading: How to Use Workflows (Push-Triggered Build)
content: To set this workflow up, first upload the project to GitHub. Then, because the project must be an Android Studio project using Gradle, create a workflow file named generate-apk-aab-debug-release.yml inside the .github/workflows/ folder. The workflow, named Generated APK AAB (Push Github - Create Artifact To Github Action), defines environment variables for the main project module (app), the Play Store display name (Frogobox ID), and a build output path (buildActionResult). It triggers on pushes to any release/** branch and can also be run manually from the Actions tab via workflow_dispatch. The job checks out the repository, records the current date and repository name as environment variables, sets up JDK 17 (Zulu distribution) with Gradle caching, makes gradlew executable, runs the Gradle test suite, builds the project, assembles the debug APK, assembles the release APK, and builds the release App Bundle (AAB) for the main module. It then uploads the debug APK, release APK, and release AAB as GitHub Actions artifacts named using the date, Play Store name, and repository name. Finally, it cleans and recreates a build_output_path directory, copies the debug APK, release APK, and release AAB into it, lists the copied files for verification, and commits and pushes those build outputs back to the GitHub repository using the workflow actor's git identity. This workflow was reported as tested successfully against a private repository.

heading: How to Use Workflows (Manual / Upload-Triggered Build)
content: A simpler variant of the workflow, named Generated APK AAB (Upload - Create Artifact To Github Action), lives at the same path (.github/workflows/generate-apk-aab-debug-release.yml) and omits the copy-and-push-to-git steps. It still triggers on pushes to release/** branches or manual dispatch, checks out the code, records the date and repository name, sets up JDK 17 with Gradle caching, makes gradlew executable, runs tests, builds the project, assembles debug and release APKs, builds the release AAB, and uploads all three as named artifacts. After pushing, the build runs automatically on the repository's Actions tab, and the resulting artifact can be downloaded from there. The article also links to the underlying GitHub Actions used for downloading artifacts, uploading artifacts, and removing old artifacts. Screenshots in the source document show successful generation of debug APK(s), release APK(s), and release App Bundle(s) against a private repository.

heading: Clean Up Artifact Workflow
content: A separate scheduled workflow, Generated APK AAB (Clean), removes old GitHub Actions artifacts automatically. It can be triggered manually via workflow_dispatch or runs on a daily cron schedule at 1am (cron expression '0 1 * * *'). The job, remove-old-artifacts, runs on ubuntu-latest with a 10-minute timeout and uses the c-hive/gha-remove-artifacts@v4 action, configured with an age threshold (the example uses '60 seconds', with the comment noting the format is '<number> <unit>', e.g. 5 days or 2 years). Optional inputs shown but commented out include skip-tags and skip-recent, which would let the cleanup skip tagged releases or a number of the most recent artifacts.

heading: Using Bundletool to Extract a Universal APK
content: For teams that want an installable APK derived directly from the release AAB, the article documents a Bundletool-based workflow. The first step is preparing Bundletool itself: the workflow expects the jar at .github/lib/bundletool.jar, with the latest release downloadable from the bundletool GitHub releases page. The second step is using the workflow code in android-ci-generate-apk-aab-upload-3.yml, which extends the earlier workflow with additional environment variables for keystore handling (ks_path, ks_store_pass, ks_alias, ks_alias_pass — shown in the source with example values). After building the release AAB, the workflow captures the generated AAB's base filename into a generated_name_aab environment variable, then invokes bundletool build-apks against that AAB to produce a device-agnostic APK set, unzips the universal.apk from that APK set, and uploads the debug APK, release APK, and release AAB as artifacts exactly as in the simpler workflows. After running the action (step 3), the user waits for it to complete, checking periodically for errors (step 4), then downloads the AAB artifact and extracts it (step 5).

heading: Run Using Gradle Configuration (.run)
content: As an alternative for developers who do not have or do not want to use GitHub Actions, the article documents running builds locally through an IntelliJ/Android Studio .run configuration. The first step is creating a .run folder at the root of the project directory, which enables running builds on the local machine. The second step is creating a file named [name-config].run.xml inside that folder, containing an XML ProjectRunConfigurationManager component that wraps a GradleRunConfiguration with an ExternalSystemSettings block specifying the external project path, the GRADLE system id, and a list of Gradle task names — the example task is :app:assembleDebug. The third step is that the configuration then appears in the IDE's run-configuration menu, and the fourth step, running it, executes the configured Gradle task(s) locally. A second sample configuration named signingreport is also provided, which runs Gradle's signingreport task to print the signing certificate fingerprints (SHA-1/SHA-256) for each build variant — useful for registering a keystore fingerprint with services such as Firebase or the Play Console.

heading: Run Using Script Files (Batch and Shell)
content: The article also provides standalone build scripts for running the same build sequence outside of Gradle run configurations or CI. A Windows batch script (run from a subfolder, navigating up to the project root) sequentially runs gradlew clean, gradlew test, gradlew build, gradlew assembleDebug, gradlew assemble, and gradlew app:bundleRelease, printing numbered progress messages ([1/6] through [6/6]) and pausing at the end. An equivalent Bash shell script performs the same six steps — clean, test, build, assembleDebug, assemble, and app:bundleRelease — with matching progress echoes, for use on macOS or Linux.

heading: Collaborator / Contributing
content: The project is open to contributions, and the maintainer, Muhammad Faisal Amir, states that contributor names will be added to the README upon request. Prospective contributors are asked to email faisalamircs@gmail.com with a subject line following the pattern Github_[Github-Username-Account]_[Language]_[Repository-Name], for example Github_amirisback_kotlin_admob-helper-implementation. At the time this document was captured, the contributor waiting list was empty.

heading: AI Agent Skill
content: The project ships an AI Agent Skill intended to help AI coding assistants, such as Antigravity, understand and manage the GitHub Workflows defined in the repository. Developers using a compatible AI assistant can point it at the skill file located at skills/github-workflows/SKILL.md. Documented example prompts include asking the assistant to explain how the APK upload workflow works using the github-workflows skill, asking it to use the skill to add a new environment variable across all CI scripts, and asking it to help troubleshoot a failed build using the skill.

heading: Related Content on Google Dev Library
content: The Dev Library page surfaces several other related Android repositories. Chucker, by ChuckerTeam, is an HTTP inspector for Android and OkHttp, comparable to Charles Proxy but running on-device. detekt, by contributor cortinico, is a static code analysis tool for the Kotlin programming language that operates on the abstract syntax tree provided by the Kotlin compiler. AboutLibraries, by mikepenz, automatically collects all dependencies and licenses of a Gradle project (including Kotlin Multiplatform projects) and provides easy-to-integrate UI components for displaying them on Android. PermissionX, by guolindev, is an open-source Android library that makes handling Android runtime permissions extremely easy. LitePal, also by guolindev, is an Android library that makes using a SQLite database extremely easy. The page footer links to the Dev Library's About, Terms, Privacy, and Feedback pages.
